h1Title: Borderline Tuberculoid Leprosy: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Borderline tuberculoid leprosy represents the mid-spectrum form of leprosy, positioned between the tuberculoid pole (strong cell-mediated immunity, few lesions) and the lepromatous pole (weak immunity, widespread disease). Patients with BT leprosy typically present with 1-3 hypopigmented or erythematous patches that show clearly reduced sensation to light touch, pinprick, and temperature — the hallmark of tuberculoid-spectrum disease. The borderline category is immunologically unstable: without treatment, BT leprosy can shift toward either pole over time. WHO multi-drug therapy for paucibacillary leprosy (6 months of rifampicin and dapsone) is highly effective, achieving cure rates above 95% and substantially reducing transmission in endemic communities.

keyStats.prevalence: BT leprosy accounts for approximately 15-20% of newly diagnosed leprosy cases globally; the borderline spectrum as a whole represents roughly 40-50% of all leprosy presentations
keyStats.demographics: BT leprosy occurs across all age groups in endemic regions; no sex predilection; children under 15 represent 10-15% of cases in high-burden countries
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at diagnosis is 30-40 years in endemic countries; younger age at diagnosis reflects active transmission
keyStats.globalCases: WHO reports approximately 200,000 new leprosy cases annually worldwide, with BT-classified cases representing a significant portion of the borderline spectrum

definition: Borderline tuberculoid (BT) leprosy (ICD-10: A30.3) is the mid-spectrum form of leprosy within the Ridley-Jopling classification, characterized by an immune response that is intermediate between the robust cell-mediated immunity of tuberculoid (TT) leprosy and the deficient immunity of lepromatous (LL) leprosy. Caused by Mycobacterium leprae, a slow-growing acid-fast bacillus transmitted primarily through prolonged respiratory contact with untreated multibacillary patients, BT leprosy manifests as 1-3 hypopigmented or erythematous patches with sharply demarcated but less distinct borders than in TT disease. Sensation is impaired within lesions but not completely absent. Slit-skin smear testing is usually negative or shows a low bacterial index (1+), distinguishing BT from multibacillary forms. The borderline immune state is inherently unstable — BT can upgrade toward TT (improving immune response) or downgrade toward BB or BL (worsening immune response) without treatment, making prompt diagnosis and MDT initiation critical for preventing complications and immunologic deterioration.

typesClassification.description: Single hypopigmented patch with well-preserved sensation loss, low bacterial index, and strong BCG scar history. Closest to the TT pole with minimal nerve involvement.
Two to three lesions with partial sensory loss, slightly more indistinct borders, and possible involvement of a peripheral nerve trunk. Bacterial index remains low.
Three to five lesions, less well-defined borders, higher bacterial index (up to 2+), and occasional satellite lesions around the main patch. Nerve involvement more common.

primarySymptoms: One to three hypopigmented or erythematous patches on the trunk, limbs, or face — lesions are flat or slightly raised with borders that are less distinct than in pure tuberculoid disease.
Reduced sensation (numbness) within patches to light touch, pinprick, and temperature — sensation loss is marked but not always complete, distinguishing BT from LL where sensation is preserved.
Dry, scaly skin within lesions — xerosis and fine scaling are common in the patch area, sometimes mistaken for eczema or fungal infection if sensation loss is not specifically tested.
Peripheral nerve thickening — the ulnar nerve at the elbow and the posterior tibial nerve near the ankle are commonly enlarged and tender on palpation in BT leprosy.
Muscle weakness in the hand or foot corresponding to affected nerve territories — patients may notice difficulty with fine finger movements (intrinsic hand muscle weakness) or foot drop.
Anhidrosis (absent sweating) over affected patches — loss of autonomic function in the lesional skin causes dry, hairless patches.
Mild allodynia or tingling (paresthesia) in and around lesions — patients may describe burning, prickling, or hypersensitivity in early BT disease.
Asymptomatic lesions discovered incidentally — patients may not notice numbness until they burn or injure the patch without feeling it.
earlyWarningSigns: Hypopigmented patch with definite sensory loss — testing light touch and pinprick sensation in the patch area compared to contralateral skin is the simplest and most reliable field test
Enlarged peripheral nerve trunk (ulnar, median, or posterior tibial) with tenderness — nerve involvement may precede or accompany skin lesions
Family history of leprosy in an endemic area — household contacts of multibacillary patients have elevated risk and should be examined
Unexplained muscle weakness or claw hand in a person from an endemic region — late presentation of nerve damage from undiagnosed BT leprosy
Slowly progressive patch that has not resolved with topical steroids or antifungal treatment — the failure to respond to standard eczema treatment should prompt consideration of leprosy
emergencySigns: Sudden painful nerve enlargement with rapid onset of weakness — possible reversal reaction requiring urgent corticosteroid treatment to prevent permanent nerve damage
New erythematous lesions with fever and systemic symptoms — possible Type 1 reaction (reversal reaction) indicating immune upgrading; requires immediate medical attention
Worsening vision or lagophthalmos (inability to close eyelids) — ocular nerve involvement in BT can progress to exposure keratitis and blindness without urgent intervention
Complete loss of sensation in hands or feet with rapid progression — advancing nerve damage with risk of injuries, burns, and eventual disability
New painful swelling of multiple nerves (multibacillary features appearing in BT patient) — possible downgrading of disease requiring urgent MDT review

causes.description: BT leprosy is caused by infection with Mycobacterium leprae, an obligate intracellular acid-fast bacillus with a generation time of 12-14 days — one of the slowest-growing human pathogens. The bacillus primarily infects skin macrophages and Schwann cells of peripheral nerves, explaining why nerve damage is a central feature. Transmission occurs via aerosol droplets from the nasal mucosa of untreated multibacillary patients; the incubation period is 2-7 years for TT/BT spectrum disease.

The hallmark of BT leprosy is an intermediate immune state — the patient mounts a partial Th1-type cell-mediated response against M. leprae, producing fewer lesions than LL (stronger immunity than LL) but more lesions than TT (weaker immunity than TT). This is reflected in the clinical picture (multiple patches) and the slit-skin smear (low but present bacteria). The borderline state reflects intermediate HLA-DR expression and intermediate cytokine profiles (IL-2, IFN-gamma at lower levels than TT).

Not all individuals exposed to M. leprae develop leprosy — genetic factors including HLA-DR2, HLA-DR3, and specific polymorphisms in the LRRK2 and IL10 genes influence susceptibility to the BT form specifically. Family clustering occurs; first-degree relatives of BT patients have elevated risk. Socioeconomic factors including poverty, overcrowding, and malnutrition increase the likelihood of developing disease after exposure.

BT leprosy is particularly susceptible to Type 1 reversal reactions — an immunologically driven acute inflammation triggered by interferon-gamma release from activated T-cells that suddenly recognizes M. leprae antigens. The reaction causes new erythema, swelling, and tenderness in existing lesions and can cause rapid nerve damage. It may be triggered by infection, vaccination, stress, or childbirth. Reversal reactions occur in 20-30% of BT patients, most commonly within the first 6 months of starting MDT.

In hyperendemic regions (South Asia, Brazil, Indonesia, parts of East Africa), continuous exposure to M. leprae in the community maintains transmission. Environmental factors — including climate, soil type, and presence of armadillos (zoonotic reservoir in the Americas) — contribute to regional variation in prevalence and clinical presentation of BT leprosy.

riskFactors.factor: Prolonged close contact with untreated multibacillary leprosy patient
riskFactors.description: Household contacts of LL or BL patients have a 5-10-fold higher risk of developing leprosy than the general population. The risk from contact with paucibacillary (BT/TT) patients is significantly lower.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to endemic region
riskFactors.description: South Asia (India, Bangladesh, Nepal, Indonesia), Sub-Saharan Africa (Nigeria, Mozambique, Tanzania), and South America (Brazil) have the highest leprosy prevalence. Travel or extended residence in these areas increases exposure risk.
First-degree relatives of leprosy patients have 2-5 times elevated risk of developing the disease. The genetic contribution is stronger in TT/BT spectrum than in LL forms.

Socioeconomic deprivation — including poor housing, inadequate ventilation, and malnutrition — is strongly associated with higher leprosy incidence. These factors likely operate through impaired host immunity rather than through transmission alone.

Conditions that impair cell-mediated immunity — including HIV infection, organ transplantation, and systemic corticosteroid use — may alter the clinical presentation of BT leprosy and increase the risk of reversal reactions.
Children under 15 in endemic areas are disproportionately affected by BT leprosy, reflecting recent transmission and the long incubation period operating against a background of developing immunity.

affectedDemographics: BT leprosy occurs across all ages but peaks in the 20-40 age group in endemic countries, reflecting the long incubation period and cumulative exposure
No significant sex difference — male and female incidence is approximately equal, unlike LL leprosy which has a male predominance
Endemic in South Asia (India accounts for over 60% of global cases), Sub-Saharan Africa, Brazil, and parts of Southeast Asia and the Pacific
In non-endemic countries (United States, Western Europe), cases are primarily diagnosed in immigrants from endemic regions and occasionally in household contacts
diagnosisOverview: BT leprosy is diagnosed clinically and confirmed by slit-skin smear testing. The clinical hallmark is 1-3 hypopigmented or erythematous patches with clearly reduced sensation — sensation testing with Semmes-Weinstein monofilaments or simple cotton wool and pinprick testing is the cornerstone of field diagnosis. Slit-skin smears are taken from the edge of active lesions, earlobes, and elbows; in BT leprosy, the bacterial index is typically 1+ to 2+ (1-10 bacilli per high-power field), distinguishing it from TT (negative) and LL (3+ to 6+). Skin biopsy with histopathology is confirmatory: BT shows granulomas with epithelioid histiocytes and Langhans giant cells, moderate lymphocytic infiltrate, and acid-fast bacilli visible on Fite stain — fewer than in LL but more than in TT. Nerve conduction studies assess the degree of peripheral nerve involvement. Lepromin skin test is positive in BT (distinguishing it from LL) but less strongly positive than in TT. The WHO operational classification (paucibacillary vs. multibacillary) places BT leprosy in the paucibacillary category (fewer than 3 lesions, negative or weakly positive smear), guiding treatment duration.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for classifying leprosy as paucibacillary or multibacillary and confirming the BT diagnosis. Smears from lesion edges, earlobes, and elbows are stained with Ziehl-Neelsen or modified Fite stain. In BT leprosy, bacterial index is typically 1+ (1-10 bacilli per 100 oil immersion fields) or 2+.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A scraping of superficial skin tissue from the slit site is spread on a glass slide, stained, and examined under microscopy. Painless in most cases; slight discomfort from the incision. Results available within hours. BT patients are usually paucibacillary (negative or 1+ BI).

diagnosticTests.purpose: The most critical clinical test for BT leprosy — patches demonstrate clearly reduced or absent sensation to light touch (cotton wool), pinprick, and sometimes temperature. Testing must be done systematically inside and outside the patch border.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-invasive. The clinician tests with cotton wool, a standardized pinprick device, and test tubes of hot and cold water. Pattern of sensory loss within patches compared to normal skin confirms the diagnosis. Can be done by any trained health worker in the field.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive diagnostic confirmation and classification of BT leprosy. Demonstrates the characteristic histopathologic features: epithelioid cell granulomas, Langhans giant cells, moderate lymphocytic infiltrate, and acid-fast bacilli on Fite stain.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A punch biopsy (4-6 mm) from the edge of an active lesion under local anesthetic. The site is closed with a suture. Results from a pathology lab within 1-2 weeks. Shows granulomatous inflammation with bacilli in BT leprosy — fewer than in LL, more than in TT.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses the patient's cell-mediated immune response to M. leprae antigens. A positive (strong) reaction suggests TT/BT immunity; negative or weak reaction suggests LL/BL immunity. Useful for classification but not for individual diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Intradermal injection of lepromin into the forearm. Read at 3-4 weeks for the late (granulomatous) reaction. BT patients typically show a positive reaction of 5-10 mm — smaller than in TT (15+ mm) but larger than in LL (negative). Test is not commercially available as a diagnostic tool.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the degree of peripheral nerve damage in BT leprosy, particularly in patients presenting with motor weakness or sensory loss. Helps establish baseline and monitor progression.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-invasive electrophysiology test lasting 30-60 minutes. Surface electrodes are placed along the course of the ulnar, median, and peroneal nerves. In BT leprosy, slowed conduction velocity and reduced amplitude in affected nerve territories are common.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Molecular detection of M. leprae DNA from slit-skin smear or biopsy specimens. More sensitive than smear for detecting low bacterial loads in BT leprosy. Used in reference laboratories and research settings.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: PCR on DNA extracted from skin smear or biopsy tissue. Available at specialized leprosy reference centers. Highly specific but limited by availability in endemic field settings.

treatmentOverview: BT leprosy is treated with WHO-recommended multi-drug therapy (MDT) for paucibacillary leprosy: rifampicin 600 mg monthly (supervised) plus daily dapsone 100 mg, for a duration of 6 months. This regimen achieves cure rates above 95% when completed in full. The key clinical challenges in managing BT leprosy are reversal reactions (Type 1 reactions) and nerve damage — both require prompt recognition and corticosteroid treatment alongside MDT. Prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day for 3-6 months is the standard treatment for moderate-to-severe reversal reactions. All patients with nerve involvement require urgent neurology or physiotherapy referral to prevent permanent disability. Contact tracing — examining household contacts and neighbors — is essential in BT leprosy to find additional cases in the community. Completed MDT for BT leprosy results in cure with no residual infectivity; patients are considered non-infectious immediately upon starting treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: WHO MDT for Paucibacillary Leprosy (Rifampicin + Dapsone, 6 months)
medicalTreatments.description: The standard WHO regimen for BT leprosy: rifampicin 600 mg once monthly (supervised) plus dapsone 100 mg daily self-administered, for 6 months. Cures over 95% of patients who complete the full course. Rifampicin is a bactericidal agent that kills the majority of viable M. leprae rapidly; dapsone is bacteriostatic and clears remaining organisms.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate above 95% with full 6-month adherence. Bacterial index declines to zero in most BT patients within 6 months. Relapse rates are very low (<1% over 5 years). Skin lesions heal gradually; nerve function may improve with treatment but pre-existing damage does not fully reverse.

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroids for reversal (Type 1) reaction
medicalTreatments.description: Prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day tapered over 3-6 months for moderate-to-severe reversal reactions in BT leprosy. Reversal reactions represent an immunologically mediated flare rather than treatment failure and do not require modification of MDT.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapidly reduces inflammation around nerves and lesions, preventing further nerve damage. Early treatment preserves motor and sensory function. Patients treated within 2 weeks of reaction onset have better functional outcomes.

medicalTreatments.name: Dapsone monotherapy (maintenance after MDT)
medicalTreatments.description: Not used as monotherapy for active BT leprosy. After completing MDT, patients require no further antimicrobial therapy but must be monitored for relapse or reversal reactions for at least 2 years.

medicalTreatments.name: Neuroprotective agents (multidose steroids for mild reactions)
medicalTreatments.description: For mild Type 1 reactions: NSAIDs and low-dose corticosteroids (prednisone 20-30 mg/day tapered over 4-6 weeks). Neuropathic pain management with gabapentin or amitriptyline for burning dysesthesia.

alternativeTreatments.name: Physiotherapy and occupational therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Essential adjunct for BT patients with nerve damage causing weakness, claw hand, or foot drop. Prevents contractures, maintains joint range of motion, and provides assistive devices. Nerve function can improve substantially with intensive rehabilitation even after MDT completion.

alternativeTreatments.name: Protective footwear and self-care training
alternativeTreatments.description: Patients with sensory loss in feet are at risk for plantar ulcers and injuries — protective footwear (MCR boots), daily foot inspection, and prompt wound care prevent infection and disability.WHO-recommended podiatric care is a cornerstone of leprosy disability management.

whySeeSpecialist: BT leprosy should be managed by an infectious disease specialist or dermatologist with leprosy experience, in coordination with a leprosy control program. A neurologist is essential when nerve damage with motor weakness is present. Reversal reactions require urgent specialist management. Physiotherapists and occupational therapists are integral to preventing disability. Social workers and stigma counselors address the profound social consequences of leprosy diagnosis.
doctorSelectionGuide: In endemic countries, leprosy specialists are typically embedded in national leprosy control programs at district or regional hospitals. In non-endemic countries, seek infectious disease or dermatology specialists with experience in mycobacterial disease. Ask about WHO MDT protocols, reversal reaction management experience, and access to physiotherapy. Many countries have leprosy reference centers (e.g., GSH in the Philippines, ALM in Brazil, Leprosy Mission globally) with specialized expertise.
preventionStrategies: Prompt diagnosis and immediate initiation of WHO MDT eliminates patient infectivity within days — early case finding in household contacts is the single most effective prevention strategy
BCG vaccination provides partial protection against leprosy — universal BCG coverage in endemic countries reduces BT and TT incidence by approximately 30-50%
Chemoprophylaxis with single-dose rifampicin for household contacts of new leprosy cases reduces new case detection by 50-60% in high-burden settings (WHO recommendation since 2018)
lifestyleModifications: Complete the full 6-month MDT course without interruption — stopping early risks relapse and the development of drug resistance
Inspect skin daily for new lesions or changes in existing patches — early detection of reversal reactions leads to better outcomes
Practice meticulous foot care if sensation is impaired in the feet — daily inspection, moisturizing, and prompt treatment of any injury or ulcer
Avoid burns and injuries to numb areas — use protective gloves in the kitchen, check water temperature before bathing, and wear protective footwear outdoors
Attend all follow-up appointments for nerve function monitoring — nerve damage is often silent and progressive without clinical review

recommended: Protein-rich foods (fish, legumes, eggs, dairy) to support immune function and tissue repair during MDT treatment
Vitamin D and calcium supplementation if on prolonged corticosteroid therapy for reversal reactions — corticosteroids accelerate bone loss
Iron-rich foods if anemia is present — some BT patients develop anemia of chronic disease
Fresh fruits and vegetables for general nutritional support and immune function
avoid: No specific food restrictions for BT leprosy — nutrition does not directly affect M. leprae susceptibility
Avoid alcohol during reversal reaction treatment if corticosteroids are prescribed — alcohol potentiates corticosteroid side effects
Avoid raw or undercooked fish in endemic areas — fish consumption has no interaction with MDT drugs but is relevant for general food safety

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate exercise is beneficial and safe during BT leprosy treatment — walking, swimming, and light cycling support general health. Patients with foot drop or hand weakness should perform nerve-specific exercises prescribed by a physiotherapist. Stretching exercises prevent contractures in affected limbs. During reversal reactions with active nerve inflammation, rest and limiting strenuous activity is advisable until the acute phase resolves.
dailyManagement: Take dapsone daily as prescribed — set a daily alarm or routine to maintain adherence
Attend monthly rifampicin supervised dose — this is typically administered at a health center or by a community health worker
Monitor for signs of reversal reaction: new redness, swelling, or tenderness in patches, or new nerve pain with weakness — seek medical attention within 24 hours of onset
Practice daily skin inspection for new patches or changes in existing lesions
Inspect feet daily for cuts, burns, or injuries — patients with sensory loss may not notice injuries that can progress to infection
Moisturize dry patches with plain petroleum jelly or emollient to prevent cracking
Perform prescribed hand and foot exercises daily if nerve damage is present
prognosis: BT leprosy has an excellent prognosis with full MDT completion — cure rates exceed 95% and patients are rendered non-infectious rapidly. The primary determinants of long-term outcome are the presence and degree of nerve damage at diagnosis and the management of reversal reactions. Patients diagnosed early with minimal nerve involvement achieve full cure without disability. Those with established nerve damage may have residual weakness or sensory loss after treatment; intensive physiotherapy can maximize functional recovery. The key long-term risk is late-onset reversal reactions, which can occur months to years after MDT completion and require ongoing vigilance. Without treatment, BT leprosy can shift toward the lepromatous pole (worsening immune deficiency, more lesions, increasing bacterial load), making early diagnosis and treatment essential.
recoveryTimeline: Skin lesions begin to fade within 1-3 months of starting MDT; most lesions are inactive and flattened by 6 months. Sensation in patches may improve gradually but often does not return fully — the nerve damage within the patch area may be permanent even though the infection is cured. Nerve function in affected peripheral nerves can improve over 6-18 months with appropriate physiotherapy, particularly for mild-to-moderate weakness. Reversal reactions, if they occur, typically appear in the first 3-6 months of treatment and require corticosteroid treatment for 3-6 months. Patients are released from treatment as cured (RFT — released from treatment) after completing 6 months of MDT and are followed for 2 years for relapse or reaction.
complications: Reversal (Type 1) reaction — acute immune-mediated inflammation causing new redness and swelling in existing lesions, tender nerve swelling, and risk of rapid permanent nerve damage if not treated with corticosteroids
Permanent nerve damage — ulnar and posterior tibial nerves are most commonly affected, causing claw hand and foot drop; residual disability is the most significant long-term consequence of BT leprosy
Downgrading to multibacillary (BB or BL) disease — without treatment, BT leprosy can shift toward the lepromatous pole, producing more lesions, higher bacterial loads, and more severe nerve damage

Plantal ulcers — sensory loss in the feet from nerve damage leads to unnoticed injuries that can become infected and cause tissue destruction; the leading cause of disability in treated leprosy patients

Eye complications — facial nerve involvement and lagophthalmos (incomplete eyelid closure) can lead to corneal exposure, drying, and ulceration — blindness is a preventable complication with proper eye care

supportResources.name: WHO Leprosy (Hansen's disease) Information
supportResources.description: WHO's central resource on leprosy epidemiology, treatment guidelines, and global control strategy. Includes the WHO MDT regimen details and country-level statistics.

supportResources.name: International Federation of Anti-Leprosy Associations (ILEP)
supportResources.description: Federation of major international NGOs working on leprosy — provides guidance on prevention of disability, rehabilitation, and stigma reduction programs globally.

supportResources.name: Leprosy Mission Global
supportResources.description: The oldest and largest leprosy-focused NGO, operating in over 30 countries. Provides clinical services, rehabilitation, and community-based stigma reduction.

confusedWithConditions.name: Vitiligo
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Vitiligo presents with completely depigmented (not hypopigmented) patches with fully intact sensation — patients feel normally in vitiligo-affected skin. BT leprosy patches have clearly reduced sensation. Slit-skin smear is negative in vitiligo. Vitiligo patches have no sensory loss whatsoever.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tinea corporis (ringworm)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Fungal infections cause scaly, itchy, circular patches with well-defined borders but fully intact sensation. BT leprosy patches are characteristically numb. KOH preparation of skin scrapings reveals fungal elements in tinea.

confusedWithConditions.name: Nummular eczema
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Eczematous patches are itchy and inflamed with intact sensation — patients report pruritus, not numbness. BT leprosy patches are characteristically non-pruritic and numb. Response to topical steroids is prompt in eczema but absent in BT leprosy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Psoriasis causes silvery-scaled plaques with well-demarcated borders and intact sensation — no numbness. BT leprosy patches show sensory loss. Distribution pattern (scalp, extensor elbows, knees in psoriasis) differs from leprosy.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberculoid Leprosy
relatedConditions.relevance: TT leprosy is the higher-immunity pole from which BT can upgrade; TT presents with a single patch, negative slit-skin smear, and strong lepromin reaction. BT is the intermediate form between TT and LL.

relatedConditions.name: Lepromatous Leprosy
relatedConditions.relevance: LL leprosy is the low-immunity pole toward which BT can downgrade without treatment; LL presents with multiple lesions, high bacterial index, and negative lepromin reaction. Preventing downgrade is a key reason to treat BT promptly.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberculosis
relatedConditions.relevance: TB and leprosy co-exist in many endemic regions; both are mycobacterial diseases with overlapping risk factors (poverty, malnutrition, HIV). TB treatment does not affect leprosy management but HIV co-infection worsens both.

faqs.question: Is borderline tuberculoid leprosy contagious?
faqs.answer: BT leprosy is less contagious than multibacillary (LL/BL) forms because patients have low bacterial loads. However, they can transmit M. leprae to contacts, and early treatment eliminates infectivity rapidly. WHO MDT renders patients non-infectious within days of starting treatment.

faqs.question: How is borderline tuberculoid leprosy treated?
faqs.answer: WHO MDT for paucibacillary leprosy: rifampicin 600 mg monthly supervised plus dapsone 100 mg daily for 6 months. This cures over 95% of patients. Reversal reactions (Type 1) require corticosteroids in addition to MDT.

faqs.question: What is the difference between BT and TT leprosy?
faqs.answer: TT (tuberculoid) leprosy is the high-immunity pole with one patch, negative slit-skin smear, and strong lepromin reaction. BT (borderline tuberculoid) is the intermediate form with 1-3 patches, low bacterial index (1+), and less distinct lesion borders.

faqs.question: Can BT leprosy be cured completely?
faqs.answer: Yes — the M. leprae infection is completely cured with 6 months of WHO MDT. However, any nerve damage present before treatment may not fully reverse. Physical therapy maximizes functional recovery after the infection is cleared.

faqs.question: What is a reversal reaction?
faqs.answer: A Type 1 (reversal) reaction is an acute immune response that causes new redness, swelling, and tenderness in existing patches and swelling of nerves. It occurs in 20-30% of BT patients, most often in the first 6 months of MDT. It requires urgent corticosteroid treatment to prevent permanent nerve damage.

faqs.question: How do you test for sensation loss in leprosy?
faqs.answer: Sensation is tested with cotton wool (light touch), a pinprick (pain), and test tubes of hot and cold water (temperature) inside and outside the patch. BT leprosy patches show clearly reduced sensation compared to surrounding skin.

faqs.question: Does BCG vaccination protect against BT leprosy?
faqs.answer: BCG vaccination provides approximately 30-50% protection against developing leprosy, including BT forms. Universal BCG coverage in endemic countries contributes to reduced leprosy incidence. Effectiveness is higher when BCG is given repeatedly (multiple-dose BCG strategy).

faqs.question: Can household contacts be given preventive treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes — WHO recommends single-dose rifampicin chemoprophylaxis for all household contacts of new leprosy cases. It reduces new case detection in contacts by 50-60%. Contact tracing is a critical component of leprosy control programs.

faqs.question: What causes the nerve damage in BT leprosy?
faqs.answer: M. leprae infects Schwann cells (the insulating cells of peripheral nerves) and triggers a granulomatous immune response. The inflammation within the nerve sheath compresses nerve fibers, causing sensory and motor dysfunction. Reversal reactions intensify this inflammation acutely.

faqs.question: Is borderline tuberculoid leprosy curable without treatment?
faqs.answer: BT leprosy does not self-resolve — without treatment, it typically remains stable for a period and may then either upgrade (improve toward TT) or, more commonly, downgrade (progress toward multibacillary BB/BL disease) over months to years, producing more lesions and greater nerve damage.

faqs.question: How long does it take for skin patches to disappear after MDT?
faqs.answer: Skin patches begin to fade within 1-3 months of starting MDT. Most become inactive and flattened by the end of the 6-month treatment course. Residual hypopigmentation may persist for months or years after cure.

faqs.question: Can BT leprosy patients donate blood?
faqs.answer: Blood donation policies vary by country. In most endemic countries, current or recent leprosy patients are not eligible to donate blood during and shortly after MDT treatment. In non-endemic countries with very low prevalence, individual assessment by blood bank medical officers applies.

faqs.question: What is the Ridley-Jopling classification?
faqs.answer: The Ridley-Jopling system classifies leprosy on a spectrum from TT (tuberculoid, high immunity) through BT, BB, and BL (borderline forms) to LL (lepromatous, low immunity). BT is the second position on this spectrum, reflecting intermediate immune response.

faqs.question: Does BT leprosy affect children?
faqs.answer: Yes — children under 15 in endemic areas represent 10-15% of new BT leprosy cases. Children may present with fewer or smaller patches than adults. BCG vaccination status should be assessed. Management of BT in children follows the same WHO MDT regimen with age-adjusted dapsone dosing.

faqs.question: What happens if a BT patient becomes pregnant?
faqs.answer: BT leprosy during pregnancy is managed with standard MDT — dapsone and rifampicin are considered safe in pregnancy. Reversal reactions may occur postpartum (within 3 months of delivery) and require prompt treatment with corticosteroids. Breastfeeding is compatible with MDT. Pregnant contacts of leprosy patients can receive rifampicin chemoprophylaxis after the first trimester.

faqs.question: What is the bacterial index in BT leprosy?
faqs.answer: BT leprosy typically has a bacterial index of 1+ (1-10 bacilli per 100 oil immersion fields on slit-skin smear) or 2+ (11-100 bacilli per 100 fields). This places BT in the WHO 'paucibacillary' category, justifying the 6-month MDT regimen.

faqs.question: How is leprosy transmitted?
faqs.answer: Leprosy is primarily transmitted via aerosol droplets from the nasal mucosa of untreated multibacillary patients. Prolonged close contact (household contact) increases risk. Armadillos are a zoonotic reservoir in the Americas. The incubation period is 2-7 years.

faqs.question: What eye problems does BT leprosy cause?
faqs.answer: BT leprosy can involve the facial nerve (causing lagophthalmos — incomplete eyelid closure) and trigeminal nerve (causing corneal anesthesia), leading to exposure keratitis and corneal ulceration. Blinding complications are preventable with daily eye care including eyelid closure exercises and artificial tears.

faqs.question: Can BT leprosy recur after treatment?
faqs.answer: Relapse after completing full MDT for BT leprosy is rare (less than 1% over 5 years). Relapse presents with new skin patches and positive slit-skin smear. Regular follow-up for 2 years after treatment completion allows early detection. If relapse occurs, a second course of MDT is recommended.

faqs.question: What is the WHO operational classification of leprosy?
faqs.answer: The WHO operational classification divides leprosy into paucibacillary (PB: 1-3 skin patches, negative or 1+ slit-skin smear) and multibacillary (MB: more than 3 patches, BI 2+ or higher). BT leprosy falls into the PB category and is treated with 6 months of MDT (rifampicin + dapsone).

faqs.question: How do you prevent disability in BT leprosy?
faqs.answer: Prevention of disability (POD) involves: early diagnosis and prompt MDT to prevent nerve damage; rapid corticosteroid treatment of reversal reactions; daily self-care for numb hands and feet (inspection, moisturizing, protective footwear); and intensive physiotherapy for established disability.

faqs.question: What is the incubation period for BT leprosy?
faqs.answer: The incubation period for BT leprosy is typically 2-7 years (median approximately 5 years), though cases have been reported with incubation periods as short as 6 months or as long as 20 years. This long incubation explains why childhood exposure often manifests as adult disease.

metaTitle: Borderline Tuberculoid Leprosy: Symptoms, Treatment & Outlook
metaDescription: Borderline tuberculoid (BT) leprosy: mid-spectrum disease with 1-3 numb patches. WHO MDT treatment, reversal reactions, nerve damage, and prognosis explained.
